medications.indication_rationale: Applied thinly to the surrounding red, itchy, eczematous skin only (not the bumps themselves) twice daily to reduce itch and discomfort of the molluscum dermatitis. A mild topical steroid is appropriate for thin flexural skin; it treats the peri-lesional eczema, not the virus.
medications.dose_route_frequency: hydrocortisone 1% cream, thin layer to the surrounding red/eczematous skin only, twice daily (as per source note)
contraindications_in_this_case: Do not apply to broken/weeping skin or over a suspected bacterial infection; apply to the surrounding eczematous skin only

medications.indication_rationale: Simple analgesia if the area is painful, as noted by the source clinician. This is a paediatric patient (~7 y, 25 kg): paracetamol dosing is weight-based and NO dose is synthesised here - the specific mg dose must be set by a pharmacist or at in-person review per the child's weight. Flagged for in-person/pharmacist dosing.
A thick barrier cream (e.g. zinc oxide) applied over the bumps before dressing physically reduces clothing friction, easing pain on walking. Supportive, non-pharmacological-grade OTC measure.
medications.dose_route_frequency: apply a thick layer of zinc oxide barrier cream over the bumps before dressing, as needed (as per source note)

medications.indication_rationale: The BOTE inflammatory flare is an immune-mediated response, NOT a bacterial infection. Systemic antibiotics are not indicated in an afebrile, systemically well child with a negative cellulitis red-flag screen; prescribing them is the key error of commission this case tests. Antibiotics are reserved only for genuine secondary bacterial cellulitis/abscess (see safety-net).
A potent steroid is not indicated on thin paediatric flexural (groin) skin; a mild agent (hydrocortisone 1%) is sufficient and safer for the peri-lesional eczema.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Keep the child's fingernails cut short and wash hands frequently; discourage scratching, squeezing or de-roofing the bumps.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Prevents autoinoculation spread to new sites and reduces the risk of introducing a true secondary bacterial infection through broken skin.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Apply a thick barrier cream over the bumps before dressing and monitor the red area daily for any change.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reduces friction pain when walking and lets the parent detect early deterioration (spreading, hot/hard redness, pus, fever) quickly.

patient_education_points.point: The sudden redness and swelling is usually a GOOD sign - it means the immune system has finally recognised the virus and is clearing it (the 'beginning of the end'). It is not a bacterial infection and does not need antibiotics.

This inflamed phase typically lasts 1-2 weeks; afterwards the bumps crust, shrink and clear over the following months. Only the itch/soreness is treated (mild steroid to the surrounding skin); the immune system does the rest.
Do not pop, squeeze or scratch the bumps - the core is contagious and squeezing spreads it to nearby skin and can cause a real infection.
Broken skin always carries a small risk of a true secondary bacterial infection - watch the area and act on the warning signs (spreading hot/hard redness, thick pus, or fever over 38 C).

follow_up_plan.interval: 2-4 weeks (routine), or sooner if warning signs appear
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Settling of the inflammatory flare and progressive resolution of the molluscum lesions; confirm no secondary bacterial infection developed.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Rapidly spreading hot/hard redness or a red streak up the leg, a fluctuant pus pocket, or fever over 38 C - seek urgent in-person/ED care, do not wait for the routine review.

must_include_items: Recognise the benign inflammatory (BOTE) phase of molluscum contagiosum rather than bacterial cellulitis
Reassure the anxious parent that the redness is an expected immune-clearance response
Recommend mild topical hydrocortisone 1% to the surrounding eczematous skin only
Explicitly advise AGAINST systemic antibiotics for this benign inflammatory phase
Advise against squeezing/de-roofing the lesions and give hygiene/autoinoculation-prevention advice
Give a clear, mandatory safety-net for evolving secondary bacterial cellulitis/abscess (spreading hot/hard redness, pus pocket, fever >38 C -> urgent in-person/ED)
acceptable_alternatives: Arranging a routine or next-day in-person GP/nurse review to lay eyes on the groin, given telehealth cannot palpate - acceptable and mildly cautious, not over-triage
Simple analgesia (paracetamol, weight-based dose set in person) for pain
A barrier cream over the bumps to reduce friction pain
errors_of_omission: Failing to give an explicit safety-net for secondary bacterial cellulitis/abscess
Failing to reassure the parent, leaving them to seek unnecessary emergency care
Not eliciting the central umbilication before committing to management
errors_of_commission: Prescribing systemic antibiotics for the benign BOTE flare
Escalating an afebrile, systemically well child with a negative red-flag screen to the emergency department (over-triage)
Advising the parent to squeeze, de-roof or otherwise remove the lesions
Recommending a potent topical steroid on thin paediatric groin skin
Synthesising a specific paediatric paracetamol dose without weight-based in-person/pharmacist confirmation
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass REQUIRES (1) NOT prescribing systemic antibiotics for the benign inflammatory phase and (2) an explicit safety-net for evolving secondary bacterial cellulitis/abscess. Over-triage to ED / urgent in-person, or antibiotic prescription, for this afebrile child with a negative red-flag screen is the primary scored defect; correct management is reassurance + topical care + safety-net.
